entry.title: Bitcoin covenants are inevitable
entry.summary:
The discussion emphasizes that the difficulty adjustment function in blockchain technology, specifically within Bitcoin, is limited to adjusting for variations in the observable, non-malicious hashing power. It clarifies that this mechanism does not respond to potential threats such as chain splits, 51% attacks, or censorship attempts. This point underscores the necessity of not attributing undue capabilities to the technological aspects of Bitcoin, highlighting the importance of realistic expectations from the technology.
Furthermore, the debate touches upon the governance of Bitcoin's block reward system and its eventual decline to zero, which poses a significant risk. The current security expenditure of Bitcoin stands at approximately 1.7% of the total coin supply per year, a figure deemed sufficient given Bitcoin’s operational stability. This rate is contrasted with economic volatility, suggesting even lower rates like 1%, 0.5%, or even 0.1% could be sustainable. Over long periods, such as 75 years, these low inflation rates translate into substantial cumulative costs on savings, yet they are likely to be overshadowed by broader economic fluctuations.
For further insights into these topics, including detailed technical discussions and perspectives on Bitcoin's operational mechanisms, one can refer to resources available on [Peter Todd's website](https://petertodd.org) or contact him directly via email at 'peter@petertodd.org'.
